text:
Short answer: **No — the litigation record did not establish that Dominion voting machines switched votes.**

More precise version, if you need wording that won’t overstate things:

- **Yes, court filings included allegations, affidavits, expert-style reports, and exhibits claiming machine irregularities or vote switching.**
- **But no, those materials did not result in a court-established finding or substantiated record showing Dominion machines actually switched votes.**

So if your binary production note is:

- **“Was there court-filed material alleging/supporting it?” → Yes.**
- **“Was it established in the litigation record as having happened?” → No.**

That distinction matters because a lot of the material was **filed by parties** as allegations or proposed evidence, but **not accepted by courts as proving the claim**. In general, courts either rejected those claims, found them unsupported, or the cases ended without any judicial finding that vote-switching occurred.

If you want a one-line formulation that is careful without sounding absolutist:

**“The cases included court-filed allegations and supporting materials claiming vote switching, but the litigation record did not establish that Dominion machines actually switched votes.”**
